title: From Clear Image to Pure Noise
concept: After enough steps (often 1000), the original image becomes indistinguishable from pure random static, with no original structure remaining.

exercises.prompt: If you keep adding drops of black ink to a glass of clear water, what happens after MANY drops?

options.text: The water turns completely black, no clear water left

exercises.explain: Exactly! Each drop adds a little noise, and after enough drops, the original clear water is completely gone — just like an image after many noise steps.

cards.title: Where Did the Picture Go?
cards.body: Imagine watching a photo of your dog slowly dissolve into TV static, one tiny sprinkle of noise at a time. After enough sprinkles... it's just fuzz. No dog, no shapes, no colors you'd recognize. Where did the image go? Let's find out!

cards.title: Total Noise Takeover
cards.body: If you add a tiny bit of random noise to an image over and over — often around 1000 times — the original picture eventually disappears completely. What's left looks exactly like pure random static, with zero traces of the original structure, colors, or shapes.

cards.title: The Melting Ice Sculpture
cards.body: Think of an ice sculpture shaped like a swan, sitting in the hot sun. At first you can still tell it's a swan, just a little melty. But keep waiting, and eventually it's just a puddle of water — no swan shape left at all. That puddle is like pure noise: all the 'swan info' is gone forever, just from little bits of melting over and over.

cards.title: Step by Step to Static
cards.body: Step 1: A clear photo of a sunset. Step 250: Colors look a bit grainy but you can still guess it's a sunset. Step 600: Only faint blobs of color remain. Step 1000: Pure random speckles — an AI (or a person) looking at it couldn't tell it was ever a sunset at all.

cards.title: Myth: 'A faint trace always remains'
cards.body: Myth: Even after 1000 noise steps, you could still find a tiny hint of the original image if you looked closely. Truth: After enough steps, the image is mathematically and visually identical to pure random noise. There is no hidden 'ghost' of the original picture left to find — it's genuinely gone.

cards.title: Start vs. End of the Noise Journey
cards.body: Compare the very beginning and the very end of the noising process.

left.text: Crisp, clear photo with recognizable shapes, edges, and colors — you know exactly what it is.

right.text: Pure random static — just scattered speckles with no shapes, edges, or meaning left at all.

exercises.prompt: After around 1000 noise steps, what does the original image become?

options.text: A blurry but still recognizable version of the original
Pure random noise with no original structure left

exercises.explain: Correct! After enough noise steps, the image becomes indistinguishable from pure random static — none of the original structure survives.

exercises.prompt: True or False: If you zoom in closely enough on the final noisy image, you can still find hints of the original picture.
exercises.explain: False! After enough steps, the original structure is completely gone. The final image is genuinely indistinguishable from random noise, no matter how closely you look.

exercises.sentence: After enough noise steps, the image becomes indistinguishable from pure random ___.
exercises.explain: 'Noise' is the key word — the image ends up looking just like random static, with no structure remaining.

exercises.prompt: Which of these best describes what happens to an image's structure over 1000 noise steps?

options.text: It gradually fades away completely, step by step, until nothing recognizable is left
It stays exactly the same the whole time
It disappears instantly after just one step

exercises.explain: Right! The structure fades gradually, a tiny bit at a time, until after enough steps it's completely gone — just like the melting ice sculpture.

exercises.prompt: A friend says: 'AI diffusion models add noise to an image, but no matter how many steps you do, you can always recover some trace of the original picture just by looking at the final noisy result.' Is your friend correct?

options.text: Yes, some visible trace always remains no matter how many steps
No, after enough steps (often 1000), the image becomes pure random noise with no original structure left
Yes, but only for black and white images
No, because noise is only added once, not repeatedly

exercises.explain: Your friend is incorrect! After enough noise steps (often around 1000), the image becomes truly indistinguishable from pure random static — no original structure, shape, or color survives that you could spot by looking.
